translatedText: Around 1062
Gained control of the neighboring region of Maine
Around 1064
Secured his control over Maine
September 1066
Organized a massive fleet and invaded England
October 14, 1066
Defeated and killed Harold at the Battle of Hastings
Christmas 1066
Crowned King of England at Westminster Abbey in London
Early 1067
Prepared for the governance of England and returned to Normandy
Suppressed the rebellion of Edwin and Morcar and built Warwick, York, and Nottingham Castles
Early 1069
Quelled the rebellion of Edgar the Ætheling's attack on York
Met with three papal legates to hold councils to reform and reorganize the English Church
Suppressed the final rebellion in the North and imprisoned Morcar
Invaded Scotland, defeated Malcolm, and signed the Treaty of Abernethy
March 30, 1073
Captured Le Mans in Maine, bringing an end to the campaign
Suppressed the 'Revolt of the Earls'
Defeated by King Philip of France at the Battle of Dol
Late 1077 or early 1078
Trouble erupted with his eldest son, Robert
January 1079
Unhorsed by Robert during the siege of Gerberoy
Around April 12, 1080
Reached an agreement with Robert, reaffirming he would receive Normandy upon William's death
Visited Wales
Ordered the arrest of his half-brother Odo
November 2, 1083
His wife, Queen Matilda, died
Christmas 1085
Ordered the compilation of the Domesday Book
August 1, 1086
Domesday Book work mostly complete and exacted the Oath of Salisbury
July 1087
Led an expedition into the Vexin in France
September 9, 1087
Died at the Priory of Saint Gervase in Rouen
Solidification of the Title of Duke of Normandy
Around 1060, after a long struggle for the dukedom, he firmly secured the territory of Normandy as his own.
Conquest of England
In 1066, he invaded England, won the Battle of Hastings, was crowned King of England, and led the Norman Conquest.
Compilation of the Domesday Book
In 1086, he ordered the compilation of the Domesday Book, a survey recording landownership across England.
Laying the Foundation for English Governance
He built multiple castles in England, settled Norman nobles there, and changed the composition of the English clergy.
Alliance with Flanders
Through his marriage to Matilda of Flanders, he gained a powerful alliance with the neighboring County of Flanders.
When was William I born?
William I was born on November 8, 1028, in Falaise, Duchy of Normandy.
How did William I become King of England?
Following the death of Edward the Confessor in 1066, he invaded England, won the Battle of Hastings, and was crowned King of England in London on Christmas Day of the same year.
What is the Domesday Book?
The Domesday Book is a survey recording the state of all landownership in England, which William I ordered to be compiled in 1086.
When did William I die?
William I died on September 9, 1087, at the Priory of Saint Gervase in Rouen while on campaign in northern France.
What was William I's family background?
He was born as an illegitimate son of Robert I, Duke of Normandy, and his mistress Herleva, and married Matilda of Flanders, with whom he had four sons and five or six daughters.
Around 576 BC or 590 BC
Born as the only son of Cambyses I, King of Anshan of Persian descent, and Mandane, daughter of Astyages, King of Media
554 BC
Rebelled against his maternal grandfather Astyages and began the war against Media
549 BC
Conquered the Median Empire by capturing Ecbatana
Around 547 BC
Marched to Sardis, the capital of Lydia
546 BC
Crushing defeat of King Croesus of Lydia and the fall of Sardis
By 542 BC
Greek Ionian cities on the Aegean coast were subjugated to Persia
539 BC
Invaded the Neo-Babylonian Empire and captured Babylon
Around August 530 BC
Killed in battle against Central Asian nomads east of the Caspian Sea
Built the Great Persian Empire
He conquered most of Southwest and Central Asia, expanding Persia into a vast empire reaching as far as India.
Conquered the Median Empire
He conquered the Median Empire by capturing Ecbatana in 549 BC.
Conquered Lydia
He conquered Lydia by capturing its capital, Sardis, in 546 BC.
Conquered the Neo-Babylonian Empire
He invaded the Neo-Babylonian Empire and captured Babylon in 539 BC.
Liberated the Jews and Permitted Temple Reconstruction
He liberated the Jewish captives in Babylon, allowing them to return to Jerusalem and rebuild their sacred Temple of Jerusalem.
Policy of Religious Tolerance
He advocated a policy of religious tolerance and inclusion to harmonize the multiethnic Persian Empire.
Cyrus Cylinder
He left behind the Cyrus Cylinder, known as the first declaration of human rights that banned slavery.
Where did the name Cyrus originate?
Ancient Greek historians recorded that the name Cyrus came from 'Kouros', meaning the sun, and it is related to the meaning 'like the sun' in Old Persian.
How is Cyrus mentioned in the Bible?
He is recorded as 'Cyrus' in the Old Testament, appearing as the king who liberated the Jews from the Babylonian captivity and permitted the rebuilding of the Jerusalem Temple.
How did Cyrus die?
According to ancient historians, he was killed in battle against Central Asian nomads east of the Caspian Sea, while Herodotus recorded that he died fighting the queen of the Massagetae.
What is the Cyrus Cylinder?
It is known as the first declaration of human rights banning slavery, and is currently housed in Room 52 of the British Museum.
Where is the tomb of Cyrus?
It is located in Pasargadae and is registered as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
August 12, 1887
He was born in Vienna, Austria.
He obtained his doctorate at the University of Vienna.
April 6, 1920
He married Annemarie (Anny) Bertel.
He moved to the University of Zurich.
January 1926
He published "Quantization as an Eigenvalue Problem", a paper on wave mechanics that included the Schrödinger equation.
He succeeded Max Planck at the Friedrich Wilhelm University in Berlin.
He left Germany in opposition to Nazism.
He won the Nobel Prize in Physics alongside Paul Dirac.
He proposed the Schrödinger's cat thought experiment.
After the annexation of Austria, he was dismissed from Graz, fled to Italy, and then moved to Dublin.
He became the Director of the School for Theoretical Physics at the Dublin Institute for Advanced Studies.
He wrote "What Is Life?".
He became a naturalized Irish citizen.
He retired in Dublin.
He returned to Vienna.
January 4, 1961
He died of tuberculosis in Vienna at the age of 73.
January 2022
The School of Physics at Trinity College Dublin recommended removing Schrödinger's name from a lecture theatre due to his history of sexual abuse.
Developed the Schrödinger Equation
He developed a number of fundamental results in quantum mechanics by formulating an equation that calculates the wave function of a system and how it dynamically changes over time.
Won the Nobel Prize in Physics
He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics jointly with Paul Dirac in 1933 for the formulation of the Schrödinger equation.
Authored "What Is Life?"
He addressed the issue of genetics by looking at life phenomena from a physics perspective, which inspired the discovery of the DNA double helix structure.
Proposed the Schrödinger's Cat Thought Experiment
A famous thought experiment devised to mock the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics, it remains one of his most enduring legacies.
